title: Hot cross buns
subtitle: Spiced yeasted buns with currants and a sticky glaze
excerpt: Lightly spiced enriched buns with mixed fruit and peel, marked with a flour cross and glazed with apricot jam straight from the oven. Traditionally made at Easter, but good enough to bake any time the spring light starts to hold.
sourceNotes: Hot cross buns are documented in English baking literature from at least the 18th century. John Twamley's 18th-century recipe records the spiced bun tradition associated with Good Friday. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg, includes a hot cross bun recipe with mixed spice, currants, and a flour cross. The proportions here follow the Acton tradition (milk-enriched, spiced, cross piped in flour paste) with contemporary technique: fast-action yeast in place of the fresh yeast Acton specifies.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked buns in a sealed bag for up to 2 months. Defrost overnight and warm in a low oven for 5 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the day before; the buns soften overnight in the bag. Warm through before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 180°C. The buns need the colour that comes with direct heat.

recipeTools.notes: for piping the cross; a small zip-lock bag with the corner cut is a workable alternative

glossaryTerms.definition: A yeast dough that contains fat (butter, oil, or eggs) in addition to flour, water, and yeast. The fat tenderises the crumb and slows staling. Brioche, hot cross buns, and Chelsea buns are all enriched doughs. Higher enrichment generally means a slower rise and a tighter crumb than a plain loaf.

content.text: : milk, butter, and egg work against the yeast, so the rise is slower than a plain loaf and the dough needs confident kneading. The spicing should be in the background; mixed spice and cinnamon together give the characteristic warmth without any single note dominating. The glaze matters. A warm apricot jam wash applied straight from the oven gives the buns their lacquered surface and a slightly sticky exterior that tastes nothing like jam.

Combine flour, yeast, sugar, salt, mixed spice, and cinnamon in a large bowl. Make a well in the centre and pour in the warm milk, melted butter, and egg. Mix to a shaggy dough, then turn out and knead for 10 minutes by hand until smooth and elastic. The dough will feel slightly sticky at first; resist adding more flour. Work in the sultanas, currants, and mixed peel until evenly distributed. Shape into a ball, return to the bowl, cover with cling film, and leave in a warm place for 60 minutes until roughly doubled.

Turn the dough out and divide into 12 equal pieces (about 100 g each). Roll each into a tight ball and place close together in a 3x4 grid on a lined baking tray. Cover loosely with oiled cling film and prove for 45 minutes until the buns have puffed and are touching each other.

For the cross paste, beat the plain flour and cold water to a smooth, pipeable paste. Fill a piping bag or a small bag with the corner cut. Pipe a cross over each bun in one continuous line across the row, then repeat perpendicular. Bake at 200°C conventional (180°C fan) for 18 to 22 minutes until deep gold. While hot, warm the apricot jam with a splash of water, sieve it, and brush over the buns. Cool on a rack for at least 15 minutes before eating.
